name: Conflict Resolution & Internal Integration
description: Drama Triangle and Internal Family Systems for resolving interpersonal and internal conflicts.
when_to_use: User is describing a relationship conflict
Agents are in disagreement about approach
Task involves internal resistance or competing priorities
User is stuck between conflicting desires or obligations

drama_triangle.name: Drama Triangle (Karpman)
drama_triangle.description: Three dysfunctional roles in conflict: Victim, Persecutor, Rescuer. Recognition is the first step to breaking free.
drama_triangle.best_for: Interpersonal conflicts, recognizing dysfunctional dynamics, communication breakdowns

victim.empowerment_shift: Creator — 'What outcome do I want, and what can I do to move toward it?'

persecutor.empowerment_shift: Challenger — 'How can I set boundaries and express needs without attacking?'

rescuer.description: Overhelping, enabling, fixing. 'Let me save you.'
rescuer.empowerment_shift: Coach — 'How can I support without disempowering? Help them build their own capability.'

empowerment_dynamic.description: The healthy alternative: Creator, Challenger, Coach
empowerment_dynamic.creator: Takes ownership of outcomes and choices
empowerment_dynamic.challenger: Provokes growth through honest feedback, not attacks
empowerment_dynamic.coach: Asks questions that help others find their own solutions

detection_questions: Is someone in this situation feeling powerless? (Victim role)
Is someone being blamed or blaming? (Persecutor role)
Is someone trying to fix things for someone who hasn't asked? (Rescuer role)
What would the empowered version of each role look like here?

ifs.name: Internal Family Systems (simplified)
ifs.description: The mind has different 'parts' with different agendas. Integration comes from understanding and respecting each part's intention.
ifs.best_for: Internal conflict, self-sabotage, competing desires, procrastination, resistance
ifs.core_concept: Every 'part' of you has a positive intention, even the ones that seem destructive. The inner critic is trying to protect you. The procrastinator is trying to avoid pain. Understanding the intention allows integration.

inner_critic.behavior: Harsh self-judgment, perfectionism, 'you're not good enough'
inner_critic.positive_intention: Trying to protect from failure and rejection by holding to high standards
inner_critic.integration: Acknowledge the protection, then ask: is this level of criticism helping or hurting?

protector.positive_intention: Trying to prevent emotional pain or overwhelm
protector.integration: What specific pain is being avoided? Is there a way to approach it safely?

achiever.behavior: Overwork, inability to rest, constant productivity
achiever.positive_intention: Trying to prove worth and create safety through accomplishment
achiever.integration: Worth isn't conditional on output. What would 'enough' look like?

rebel.behavior: Resistance to structure, breaking commitments, defiance
rebel.positive_intention: Trying to preserve autonomy and authenticity
rebel.integration: What freedom is being sought? Can we build it into the plan rather than fighting it?

process.name: Identify
process.prompt: What conflicting desires or impulses are present? Name them.

process.name: Understand
process.prompt: What is each part trying to protect or achieve? What's the positive intention?

process.name: Appreciate
process.prompt: Acknowledge that each part is trying to help, even if the method is counterproductive.

process.name: Negotiate
process.prompt: Is there a path that honors what each part needs? What would a both/and solution look like?

process.name: Integrate
process.prompt: Commit to an action plan that addresses the core needs of all parts.
